(**PSU**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), United States. It was founded in 1946 as a post-secondary educational institution for [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [veterans](/wiki/Veteran "Veteran"). It evolved into a four-year college over the next 20 years and was granted university status in 1969. It is one of two public universities in Oregon that are in a large city. It is governed by a board of [trustees](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee"). PSU is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R2: Doctoral Universities – High research activity".[[5]](#cite_note-carnegie-5)

in June 1946, founded by [Stephen Edward Epler](/wiki/Stephen_Edward_Epler "Stephen Edward Epler"), a native of [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). Epler graduated from [Cotner College](/wiki/Cotner_College "Cotner College") in [Lincoln, Nebraska](/wiki/Lincoln,_Nebraska "Lincoln, Nebraska"), and later [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), before joining the army to fight in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) After returning to the United States after serving, Epler became a veterans' counselor in Oregon's General Extension Division in Portland.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) The Vanport Extension Center was conceived by Epler in order to satisfy the demand for higher education in Portland for returning World War II veterans, taking advantage of the [G.I. Bill](/wiki/G.I._Bill "G.I. Bill").

between December 1951 and February 1952.[[9]](#cite_note-die-9) In 1955, the Center changed its name to **Portland State College** to mark its maturation into a four-year degree-granting institution,[[9]](#cite_note-die-9)[[11]](#cite_note-11) although severe restrictions were placed on the college's curriculum and growth.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) Epler, who had campaigned for a presidency role at the college, was not elected by the State Board. Without an administrative stake in the college, Epler left and accepted presidency at [Reedley College](/wiki/Reedley_College "Reedley College") in California.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) By 1956, the veteran population at the college had subsided, and baby food was no longer stocked in the bookstore.[[7]](#cite_note-youngpsu-7)

**Portland State University** (**PSU**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), United States. It was founded in 1946 as a post-secondary educational institution for [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") [veterans](/wiki/Veteran "Veteran"). It evolved into a four-year college over the next 20 years and was granted university status in 1969. It is one of two public universities in Oregon that are in a large city. It is governed by a board of [trustees](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee"). PSU is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R2: Doctoral Universities – High research activity".[[5]](#cite_note-carnegie-5)

Portland State comprises seven constituent colleges, offering undergraduate degrees in 123 fields and [postgraduate](/wiki/Postgraduate "Postgraduate") degrees in 117 fields. As of 2023, the university had a total enrollment of approximately 21,000 students. Its athletic teams are known as the [Portland State Vikings](/wiki/Portland_State_Vikings "Portland State Vikings"), with school colors of green and white. They compete at the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level, primarily in the [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1946–1964: Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1946–1964: Establishment")]

Portland State University was established as the **Vanport Extension Center** in June 1946, founded by [Stephen Edward Epler](/wiki/Stephen_Edward_Epler "Stephen Edward Epler"), a native of [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). Epler graduated from [Cotner College](/wiki/Cotner_College "Cotner College") in [Lincoln, Nebraska](/wiki/Lincoln,_Nebraska "Lincoln, Nebraska"), and later [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), before joining the army to fight in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) After returning to the United States after serving, Epler became a veterans' counselor in Oregon's General Extension Division in Portland.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) The Vanport Extension Center was conceived by Epler in order to satisfy the demand for higher education in Portland for returning World War II veterans, taking advantage of the [G.I. Bill](/wiki/G.I._Bill "G.I. Bill").

The first classes were held in the Vanport Junior High School and given its location in the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") floodplain was promptly given the colloquial title, "The U by the Slough."[[7]](#cite_note-youngpsu-7) This first summer session had 221 students, and tuition and fees were $50. Over 1,410 students registered for the 1946 fall term, which was delayed until October 7, 1946, due to a lack of space. Since the population in [Vanport](/wiki/Vanport,_Oregon "Vanport, Oregon") was decreasing after World War II, the extension center was able to use buildings created for other purposes: two childcare centers, a recreation building with three classrooms, and a shopping center, which required substantial modification to house a library, offices, and six classrooms. In addition to Vanport Junior High School,[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8) [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_High_School_(Portland,_Oregon) "Lincoln High School (Portland, Oregon)") and [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_High_School_(Portland,_Oregon) "Jefferson High School (Portland, Oregon)") high schools were used after school hours, as well as the [University of Oregon](/wiki/University_of_Oregon "University of Oregon")'s dental and medical schools, located in Portland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Lincoln_High_School_Portland_Oregon_1920.jpg/250px-Lincoln_High_School_Portland_Oregon_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_High_School_Portland_Oregon_1920.jpg)

Lincoln Hall c. 1920. Then a high school, it now serves as the university's theatre and performing arts center.

Following the May 30 [Vanport Flood of 1948](/wiki/Vanport,_Oregon#Flood "Vanport, Oregon"), the college became known as "the college that wouldn't die" for refusing to close after the flood.[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8)[[9]](#cite_note-die-9) The term was coined by Lois Hennessy, a student who wrote about the college and the flood in the *[Christian Science Monitor](/wiki/Christian_Science_Monitor "Christian Science Monitor")*,[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8) though students nicknamed the school "The college without a future."[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8) (Hennessy was the mother of poet [Gary Snyder](/wiki/Gary_Snyder "Gary Snyder").) The school occupied [Grant High School](/wiki/Grant_High_School_(Portland,_Oregon) "Grant High School (Portland, Oregon)") in the summer of 1948,[[10]](#cite_note-thesis-10) then to hastily converted buildings at the Oregon Shipyard,[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8) known as the Oregon Ship.[[9]](#cite_note-die-9) In 1953,[[8]](#cite_note-first25-8) the school moved to downtown Portland and occupied the vacated buildings of Lincoln High School on SW Broadway Street, including [Lincoln Hall](/wiki/Lincoln_Hall_(Portland,_Oregon) "Lincoln Hall (Portland, Oregon)"), then known as "Old Main."[[7]](#cite_note-youngpsu-7)

The school changed its name to the **Portland State Extension Center** between December 1951 and February 1952.[[9]](#cite_note-die-9) In 1955, the Center changed its name to **Portland State College** to mark its maturation into a four-year degree-granting institution,[[9]](#cite_note-die-9)[[11]](#cite_note-11) although severe restrictions were placed on the college's curriculum and growth.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) Epler, who had campaigned for a presidency role at the college, was not elected by the State Board. Without an administrative stake in the college, Epler left and accepted presidency at [Reedley College](/wiki/Reedley_College "Reedley College") in California.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6) By 1956, the veteran population at the college had subsided, and baby food was no longer stocked in the bookstore.[[7]](#cite_note-youngpsu-7)

### 1965–2000: Expansion and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1965–2000: Expansion and development")]

Architecture at the university was a topic of controversy in its early stages. In 1968, incoming university president [Gregory Wolfe](/wiki/Gregory_Baker_Wolfe "Gregory Baker Wolfe") commented that the buildings were distressing evidence of [Stalinist cubism](/wiki/Stalinist_architecture "Stalinist architecture") on campus, although [urban renewal](/wiki/Portland_Development_Commission "Portland Development Commission") chairman Ira Keller found them to be "perfectly lovely."[[12]](#cite_note-12) Portland State University's growth for the next couple of decades was restricted under the Oregon University System's 1929 ruling that no public university or college in Oregon could duplicate the programs offered by another, with grandfathered exclusions for the [University of Oregon](/wiki/University_of_Oregon "University of Oregon") and [Oregon State University](/wiki/Oregon_State_University "Oregon State University").[[13]](#cite_note-osb_policies-13)
Nevertheless, graduate programs were added in 1961 and [doctoral programs](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") were added in 1972.[[2]](#cite_note-pdx.edu-2) The institution was granted university status by the [Oregon State Board of Higher Education](/wiki/Oregon_State_Board_of_Higher_Education "Oregon State Board of Higher Education") in 1969, becoming Portland State University.

In 1993, PSU did away with the traditional undergraduate distribution system and adopted a new interdisciplinary general education program known as University Studies. The University Studies curriculum consists of one year of required freshman inquiry courses followed by a year of sophomore inquiry, junior cluster courses (which serve as upperclassmen [electives](/wiki/Electives "Electives")) and, finally, a [senior capstone](/wiki/Capstone_course "Capstone course"); the senior capstone course serves as a "culmination of the University Studies program," and requires students to take part in a community-based project of their choosing, often followed by a public presentation on their experience in the project.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The program garnered national attention for its learning communities, service-learning, senior capstones, and successful retention of first-year students.[[15]](#cite_note-15) *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* has on multiple occasions listed University Studies as a "Program to Look For". In 1995, two years before his death, the university honored Stephen Epler for his contributions to the university's origins.[[6]](#cite_note-stephen-6)

### 2001–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2001–present")]

In 2003 Portland State was approved to award degrees in [Black Studies](/wiki/Africana_studies "Africana studies"). That same year the university opened a center housed in the Native American Student and Community Center. In 2004 the College of Engineering and Computer Science was renamed the Maseeh College of Engineering and Computer Science, after an alumnus [Fariborz Maseeh](/wiki/Fariborz_Maseeh "Fariborz Maseeh").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In August 2020, citing the [George Floyd protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests") as well as the killing of a Black man by PSU ([campus police](/wiki/Campus_police "Campus police")) officers in 2018, university President [Stephen Percy](/wiki/Stephen_Percy "Stephen Percy") announced that the campus police will no longer carry guns on patrol (though firearms will still be stored in the public safety office for use in case of an [active shooter](/wiki/Active_shooter "Active shooter") situation). In case of dangerous calls, [Portland police](/wiki/Portland_Police_Bureau "Portland Police Bureau") will respond instead.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) The policy took effect on September 1, 2021.[[19]](#cite_note-19) However, this policy was later reversed, and as of early 2023 campus police have resumed carrying weapons.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Shattuck_Hall-2.jpg/250px-Shattuck_Hall-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shattuck_Hall-2.jpg)

[Shattuck Hall](/wiki/Shattuck_Hall "Shattuck Hall"), home to the university's school of architecture

Portland State offers undergraduate degrees in one hundred twenty-three fields, and postgraduate degrees in one hundred and seventeen. The university has increasingly added more [doctoral](/wiki/Doctoral "Doctoral") programs as it has grown from its original mission as a liberal arts undergraduate college into a more broad-based research university. Recently added fields where doctorates are awarded are mathematics, biology, chemistry, applied physics, computer science, applied psychology, engineering & technology management, mechanical engineering, and sociology. Graduate education is now offered in more than 70 master's degree programs, more than 30 graduate certificate programs, and 20 doctoral programs.

In 2006, the College of Urban and Public Affairs established Portland State University's first fully online degree.[[23]](#cite_note-http-23) The Division of Criminology and Criminal Justice offers an online bachelor's degree in criminology and criminal justice as well as certificates in Advanced Crime Analysis, Criminal Behavior, Leadership in Criminal Justice, and a post-baccalaureate certificate in Criminology and Criminal Justice. Portland State awarded a total of 6,050 degrees for the 2014–15 academic year, including 4,250 bachelor's degrees, 1,725 master's degrees and 75 doctoral degrees.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Admissions")]

According to the *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* and *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*, the university's acceptance rate was 95% in 2012,[[25]](#cite_note-25) which was considered selective for a state university.[[26]](#cite_note-us_news-26) According to *Forbes* in their 2015 survey, the university's acceptance rate was 61%.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Portland State also has a dual enrollment agreement with [Portland Community College](/wiki/Portland_Community_College "Portland Community College") and [Clackamas Community College](/wiki/Clackamas_Community_College "Clackamas Community College") that allows students of the two schools to take courses at either school,[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) and also complies with the Associate of Arts Oregon Transfer Degree curriculum (A.A.O.T.), which allows accepted students who have completed two year [associate degrees](/wiki/Associate_degree "Associate degree") at an Oregon community college to transfer into the university at junior level.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Undergraduate curriculum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Undergraduate curriculum")]

#### University Studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: University Studies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Portland_State_Sky_Bridge.png/250px-Portland_State_Sky_Bridge.png)](/wiki/File:Portland_State_Sky_Bridge.png)

The university's motto on a campus skybridge over [SW Broadway St.](/wiki/Broadway_(Portland,_Oregon) "Broadway (Portland, Oregon)")

In 1993, PSU comprehensively reformed its undergraduate curriculum with a new curriculum called University Studies that is unique to the institution. The curriculum was conceived to address issues of credit distribution which required students in upper-level courses to enroll in classes outside of their majors.[[32]](#cite_note-reform-32) In a 1993 summary report on the reform, it was stated that the University Studies sought to incorporate "'across-the-curriculum' themes including writing, diversity and multiculturalism, [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics"), and [global studies](/wiki/Global_studies "Global studies")," as well as form a foundation that "includes the capacity and the propensity to engage in inquiry and critical thinking, to use various forms of communication for learning and expression, to gain an awareness of the broader human experience and its environment, and appreciate the responsibilities of persons to themselves, to each other, and to community."[[32]](#cite_note-reform-32)

PSU's University Studies curriculum begins with *Freshman Inquiry* courses, which are interactive and theme-based, and "explore topics and issues using an interdisciplinary approach to show how they can be understood from different perspectives."[[33]](#cite_note-our_program-33)

The *Sophomore Inquiry* courses are heavily communication-based, and are focused on group dialogue as well as presentations and research projects.[[33]](#cite_note-our_program-33)

As students transition into junior level, they are required to enroll in Upper Division *Cluster Courses* which are more in-depth and focused, as they pertain more closely to the students' chosen majors.[[33]](#cite_note-our_program-33) Unlike the inquiry courses that make up students' freshmen and sophomore years, the upper-division courses do not feature mentor sessions. The "clusters" from which students choose their courses cover a wide range of disciplines and themes.[[33]](#cite_note-our_program-33)

During their senior year, while still completing upper-division *Cluster Courses*, students are also required to complete a six-credit [senior capstone project](/wiki/Capstone_course "Capstone course") in order to graduate. The capstone integrates class work with community-based work. These projects are integrated with local community organizations, and cover a wide range of issues, from [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") to [grantwriting](/wiki/Grantwriting "Grantwriting"), [environmental conservation](/wiki/Environmental_protection "Environmental protection"), youth education, and more. Capstone courses often conclude with a [public presentation](/wiki/Public_speaking "Public speaking") from the students on their experiences with the community organization or cause which they explored.[[33]](#cite_note-our_program-33)

The university received national recognition for the program from the *U.S. News & World Report*, the [W.K. Kellogg Foundation](/wiki/W.K._Kellogg_Foundation "W.K. Kellogg Foundation"), the [Corporation for National Service](/wiki/Corporation_for_National_Service "Corporation for National Service"), the [Atlantic Foundation](/wiki/Atlantic_Philanthropies "Atlantic Philanthropies"), and the [Pew Charitable Trust](/wiki/Pew_Charitable_Trust "Pew Charitable Trust") for the innovative pedagogical approach to undergraduate education.[[34]](#cite_note-capstone-34)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Research")]

Portland State University is classified among “R2: Doctoral Universities – High research activity” by the Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education, reflecting levels of research expenditures and doctoral production reported through the National Science Foundation’s Higher Education Research and Development (HERD) Survey.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

In 2025, the Oregon Legislature designated Portland State University as Oregon’s Urban Research University, formally recognizing its role as the state’s primary public research institution embedded in a major metropolitan center.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Research at PSU emphasizes applied, interdisciplinary, and community‑engaged scholarship, with particular attention to urban systems, sustainability, public policy, and workforce development. Faculty research is frequently conducted in partnership with local, state, federal, and nonprofit organizations throughout the Portland metropolitan region, reflecting the university’s urban mission.

Several academic units account for a significant share of the university’s research activity. The School of Social Work is nationally recognized and ranked among the top social work programs in the United States, and its affiliated research centers — including the Regional Research Institute and the Center for Improvement of Child and Family Services — together account for approximately one‑fifth of the university’s externally funded research, with an average annual research budget of about $11 million.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Research in the Maseeh College of Engineering and Computer Science emphasizes applied and interdisciplinary work in areas such as infrastructure, transportation, environmental systems, computing, and resilience in urban environments. The School of Business conducts applied research in fields including organizational behavior, real estate, supply chains, and sustainable organizations, often in collaboration with regional industry partners.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Campus")]

See also: [List of Portland State University buildings](/wiki/List_of_Portland_State_University_buildings "List of Portland State University buildings")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Portland_State_University_Campus_OR_-_OpenStreetMap.png/250px-Portland_State_University_Campus_OR_-_OpenStreetMap.png)](/wiki/File:Portland_State_University_Campus_OR_-_OpenStreetMap.png)

Campus map

The majority of the PSU campus is located across a 50-acre (20 ha) section of southwest [downtown Portland](/wiki/Downtown_Portland,_Oregon "Downtown Portland, Oregon"), in an area known as the University District. The campus is situated against the [West Hills](/wiki/Tualatin_Mountains "Tualatin Mountains"), and is bound by Clay Street to the north, Fourth Avenue to the east, [Interstate 405](/wiki/Interstate_405_(Oregon) "Interstate 405 (Oregon)") to the south, and 12th Avenue to the west. [SW Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Portland,_Oregon) "Broadway (Portland, Oregon)") runs through the center of the campus, where the university's central buildings are located: Lincoln Hall, Cramer Hall, Smith Memorial Student Union, Fariborz Maseeh Hall, and Shattuck Hall; Cramer Hall, Smith Memorial, and Fariborz Maseeh Hall are connected by tunnels on the basement levels, as well as by [skybridges](/wiki/Skyway "Skyway") on the upper levels, which allows students access between buildings without having to use street sidewalks.

The university's [South Park Blocks](/wiki/South_Park_Blocks "South Park Blocks"), situated on the opposite side of the central buildings, run parallel to Park Avenue, and begin at Market Street where Lincoln Hall is located, and end at Shattuck Hall. The northern edge of the PSU campus is eight blocks away from [Pioneer Courthouse Square](/wiki/Pioneer_Courthouse_Square "Pioneer Courthouse Square"), and four blocks from the [Portland Art Museum](/wiki/Portland_Art_Museum "Portland Art Museum"). The [Keller Auditorium](/wiki/Keller_Auditorium "Keller Auditorium") is located at the northwestern edge of the campus, on 3rd Ave. and Clay St.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Simon_Benson_House-1.jpg/250px-Simon_Benson_House-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Simon_Benson_House-1.jpg)

[Simon Benson House](/wiki/Simon_Benson_House "Simon Benson House"), alumni and visitors' center

In 2010, the university opened a $62 million Gold [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) Certified Student Rec Center.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The six-story building houses an aquatics center, climbing wall, basketball/volleyball/badminton courts, an indoor soccer court, a large fitness area, and an outdoor program; it is located in the university's Urban Center, a [quadrangle](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)") which is also home to the College of Urban and Public Affairs, the university bookstore, and several restaurants; the [Portland Streetcar](/wiki/Portland_Streetcar "Portland Streetcar") runs west through the center.

The student-managed PSU Film Committee operates the [5th Avenue Cinema](/wiki/5th_Avenue_Cinema "5th Avenue Cinema"), one of the only student operated theaters in the United States.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Residence halls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Residence halls")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/St._Helens_Courtyard.jpg/250px-St._Helens_Courtyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Helens_Courtyard.jpg)

[St. Helens Court](/wiki/St._Helens_Court_(Portland_State_University) "St. Helens Court (Portland State University)"), residence hall at Portland State University

PSU houses around 3,000 undergraduate and graduate students, and has ten residence halls.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Its largest include University Pointe, a sixteen-story apartment building operated by [American Campus Communities](/wiki/American_Campus_Communities "American Campus Communities") built in 2011, and Ondine, a fifteen-story high rise.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Older residence halls, many of which were originally apartment buildings that were purchased by the university, include [Blackstone](/wiki/Blackstone_Hall_(Portland_State_University) "Blackstone Hall (Portland State University)"), built in 1930, and Montgomery Court, built in 1916. Other older residence halls include [St. Helens Court](/wiki/St._Helens_Court_(Portland_State_University) "St. Helens Court (Portland State University)"), built in 1927; the [art deco](/wiki/Art_deco "Art deco") [Parkway Manor](/wiki/Parkway_Manor_(Portland_State_University) "Parkway Manor (Portland State University)"), built in 1931; and Blumel Hall, built in 1986.

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Shattuck_Hall_Ecological_Learning_Plaza.jpg/250px-Shattuck_Hall_Ecological_Learning_Plaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shattuck_Hall_Ecological_Learning_Plaza.jpg)

Shattuck Hall Ecological Learning Plaza

The university has made great efforts to make its buildings environmentally sustainable, both in its new architecture as well as through renovation of its older buildings. In September 2008 the James F. and Marion L. Miller Foundation awarded Portland State University a $25 million challenge grant. The grant and the funds raised to match it must be used exclusively for sustainability programs.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit), eight buildings on the PSU campus are [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design")-certified, two of which are at Platinum status, and the university announced plans for renovations on Neuberger Hall to bring it to LEED certification as well in 2014.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Portland State has been named among the most eco-friendly universities in the United States.[[69]](#cite_note-green-69) In addition to the university's eco-conscious architecture and reconstructive work, it has also been recognized for its utilization of mass transit, including light rail, streetcar, and bus systems all central to the campus. It has also been recognized for its abundance of bicycle transportation; in 2013, PSU was ranked one of America's six most bike-friendly universities, third to [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") and [University of California, Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis").[[70]](#cite_note-bike-70)

Outside [Shattuck Hall](/wiki/Shattuck_Hall "Shattuck Hall"), the university's architecture department constructed the Shattuck Hall Ecological Learning Plaza, a garden featuring [green walls](/wiki/Green_wall "Green wall"), [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panel "Solar panel"), and permeable pavement.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Student body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Student body")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/PSU-HGCDC.jpg/250px-PSU-HGCDC.jpg)](/wiki/File:PSU-HGCDC.jpg)

The Helen Gordon Child Development Center offers preschool for children of Portland State students and faculty.

Portland State differs from the other universities in Oregon partially because, as an urban institution, it attracts a student body older than other universities;[[73]](#cite_note-75) in the 2010–2011 school year, it was reported that the average age of an attending undergraduate student was 26 years. Some programs only offer night classes. PSU also delayed the development of its campus for decades after its founding. The institution sold land in a neighboring block soon after its move to downtown Portland, and delayed the construction of student housing until the early 1970s.

The [student government](/wiki/Students%27_union "Students' union") is the Associated Students of Portland State University (ASPSU). In addition to a student body President and Vice President, there is a Student Fee Committee, a 25-member Student Senate, and a Judicial Board which rules on ASPSU constitutional questions. There are also a number of university committees that have student members appointed by the ASPSU President.[[74]](#cite_note-76) Portland State also participates in the [Oregon Student Association](/wiki/Oregon_Student_Association "Oregon Student Association"), the statewide student lobbying non-profit.

### Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Publications")]

The fully student-run [newspaper](/wiki/Student_newspaper "Student newspaper") at Portland State is the *[Portland State Vanguard](/wiki/Daily_Vanguard "Daily Vanguard")*, established in 1946. Student-run broadcasters run radio station KPSU which is ranked in the Top 20 College Radio Stations by several organizations and is one of only a handful of "Free Format" radio stations in the country, and television station PSU TV. *The Portland Review* is a literary magazine of poetry, fiction, and art published by PSU's Student Publications Board since 1956.[[75]](#cite_note-77) Additional [student newspapers](/wiki/Student_newspaper "Student newspaper") at PSU were *The Rearguard*, a monthly newspaper, and *The Spectrum.*[[76]](#cite_note-78) Following budget cuts for these publications, they were consolidated into a monthly magazine *The Pacific Sentinel* in January 2016.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Portland_Street_Car.jpg/250px-Portland_Street_Car.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portland_Street_Car.jpg)

[Portland Streetcar](/wiki/Portland_Streetcar "Portland Streetcar") at the university's [Urban Plaza](/wiki/Urban_Plaza "Urban Plaza")

The university contains four parking structures for automobiles: two located on 6th Avenue; one on 12th Street at the northwestern edge of the campus; and one 5th Avenue between Montgomery and Harrison Streets. A guest parking lot is located on the south end of [Shattuck Hall](/wiki/Shattuck_Hall "Shattuck Hall").

Portland State University is serviced with mass transit by [TriMet](/wiki/TriMet "TriMet"), which includes fifteen bus lines as well as the [MAX light rail](/wiki/MAX_light_rail "MAX light rail") system.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

There are also shuttles available through [Oregon Health & Science University](/wiki/Oregon_Health_%26_Science_University "Oregon Health & Science University") and [Portland Community College](/wiki/Portland_Community_College "Portland Community College") on SW Harrison Street at SW Broadway. In addition to use of mass transit, the university also has a large population of students who travel by [bicycle](/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle").[[70]](#cite_note-bike-70)

### Religious services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religious services")]

PSU's campus is home to two [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian") churches.

St. Michael the Archangel Catholic Church is home to the Portland State [Newman Center](/wiki/Newman_Center "Newman Center").[[83]](#cite_note-85) [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") operates an [Institute of Religion](/wiki/Institute_of_Religion "Institute of Religion") which holds Sunday services.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Other clubs for [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian"), [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), and [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") students meet on campus.[[85]](#cite_note-87)[[86]](#cite_note-88)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Portland_State_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Athletics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Jan30-2020_Viking_Pavilion.jpg/250px-Jan30-2020_Viking_Pavilion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jan30-2020_Viking_Pavilion.jpg)

The [Viking Pavilion](/wiki/Viking_Pavilion "Viking Pavilion") is home to Vikings basketball and volleyball.

Main article: [Portland State Vikings](/wiki/Portland_State_Vikings "Portland State Vikings")

Portland State competes at the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level in football, basketball, women's volleyball, golf, soccer, tennis, softball, indoor and outdoor track and field, and cross country as a member of the [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference"). The [football team](/wiki/Portland_State_Vikings_football "Portland State Vikings football") competes in [Division I FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") (Football Championship Subdivision).

Prior to joining Division I, the school won NCAA National Division II championships in women's volleyball and wrestling. The university has 30 student-managed club sports on campus including the PSU Rugby Club, the PSU Ice Hockey Club and the PSU Lacrosse Club. In addition, the Student Activities and Leadership Program sponsors 120 student clubs including the Tango, Fencing, Medieval and Brewers' clubs.

Portland State's colors are green and white, and its mascot is the Viking personified as "Victor E. Viking".

Home games for football are held off-campus at [Hillsboro Stadium](/wiki/Hillsboro_Stadium "Hillsboro Stadium"), and home games for basketball are held on-campus in the [Viking Pavilion](/wiki/Viking_Pavilion "Viking Pavilion").
